ontology.foodex2_description: Agave syrup — processed liquid sweetener derived from agave plant sap
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for agave syrup as a food substance; SNOMED CT coverage for plant-derived syrups is limited.

main_ingredients: agave plant sap (Agave tequilana or related species)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of agave syrup is approximately 1 tablespoon (21 g), commonly used as a sweetener in beverages, baked goods, or drizzled over food.

nutrient_source.title: Agave syrup — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for agave syrup (cr_id F13030004). All per-100g values preserved from the input record which carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Agave syrup GI of approximately 19 (low GI) is reported in peer-reviewed GI tables and the University of Sydney GI Database, reflecting its high fructose content which is metabolised hepatically rather than raising blood glucose acutely. GL per serving calculated as: GI (19) × carbohydrate per serving (76.37 g/100g × 21 g serving = 16.04 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.0; rounded to 3.0. Note: some published sources cite GI range 10–27 for agave syrup; 19 is used as the central estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (68 g/100g) with negligible fibre (0.2 g/100g) and negligible potassium (4 mg/100g); concentrated fructose drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction despite low sodium (4 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid agave syrup; its concentrated fructose load promotes insulin resistance and sodium retention, counteracting blood pressure management regardless of its low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 68 g/100g (predominantly fructose 55–90%) far exceeds the diabetes sugar threshold (>20 g/100g); high fructose intake promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, insulin resistance, and dyslipidaemia independent of acute glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes must avoid agave syrup; despite its low GI of approximately 19, the very high fructose content worsens hepatic insulin resistance and liver fat accumulation with regular use — stevia or erythritol are preferred zero-calorie alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High fructose intake (68 g sugar/100g, predominantly fructose) is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, increased LDL particle size, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease — all established cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should eliminate agave syrup from their diet; its concentrated fructose content raises triglycerides and promotes atherogenic dyslipidaemia, increasing cardiovascular risk beyond what sodium content alone would suggest.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 68 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory, promoting TNF-alpha, IL-6, and uric acid production; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, or meaningful fibre) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid agave syrup entirely; its high fructose content elevates uric acid and promotes systemic inflammation, worsening joint disease with no compensating anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Agave syrup (also called agave nectar) is a processed liquid sweetener derived from the sap of agave plants, primarily Agave tequilana (blue agave) and related species native to Mexico. The sap is extracted, filtered, and heated to hydrolyse complex fructans into simple sugars, producing a thick, amber-coloured syrup with a mild, neutral-to-slightly-caramel sweetness that is approximately 1.4–1.6 times sweeter than table sugar. Per 100 g, agave syrup provides 310 kcal, 76.4 g carbohydrate (of which 68 g is sugar, predominantly fructose at 55–90%), 0.45 g fat, 0.09 g protein, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and only 4 mg sodium. Its glycemic index is approximately 19 (low GI), reflecting the hepatic metabolism of fructose rather than direct blood glucose elevation. However, this low GI is clinically misleading: the very high fructose load promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, insulin resistance, elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease with regular consumption. Agave syrup is marketed as a 'natural' or 'healthier' alternative to table sugar, but clinical evidence does not support its use in diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis management. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is used globally as a sweetener in beverages, baked goods, dressings, and desserts.
common_user_queries: Is agave nectar better than sugar for diabetes?
Can I use agave syrup if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: sweetener in hot and cold beverages
baking substitute for sugar or honey

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F13030004) at confidence 0.95 with reviewed provenance. GI value sourced from peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) and University of Sydney GI Database. All values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for agave syrup (NDB No. 19912).
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; agave syrup contains negligible saturated fat by composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product containing no animal-derived components. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB source entry for this food. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 19912) confirms negligible values for most micronutrients in agave syrup. GL per serving (21 g) calculated as: 19 × (76.37 × 0.21) / 100 = 19 × 16.04 / 100 = 3.05, rounded to 3.0. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6. Agave syrup originates from Mexico and is used globally; local_names fields are null by design.
